# Krebs Cycle

The Krebs cycle (also called the citric acid cycle or TCA cycle) is a series of eight
chemical reactions that aerobic organisms use to generate energy through the oxidative
decarboxylation of acetyl-CoA.

Each full turn of the cycle processes one acetyl-CoA molecule and produces:

- **3 NADH** — fed into the electron transport chain
- **1 FADH2** — fed into the electron transport chain
- **1 GTP** (equivalent to 1 ATP)
- **2 CO2** — released as waste

Since two acetyl-CoA molecules are generated per glucose molecule (via pyruvate
decarboxylation), the Krebs cycle contributes 6 NADH, 2 FADH2, and 2 GTP per glucose.
